speaker: seller

args.message: Hey there! You've got a great eye — this 2016 Tahoe LT is one of the cleanest full-size SUVs we've had come through in months. Leather seats, Bose audio, third-row seating, and that bulletproof 5.3L V8 under the hood. Only 142,000 miles and it's been dealer-serviced its whole life. At $24,900, this thing won't last the weekend — I actually just had a guy call about it this morning. You looking for something for the family?

text: Hey there! You've got a great eye — this 2016 Tahoe LT is one of the cleanest full-size SUVs we've had come through in months. Leather seats, Bose audio, third-row seating, and that bulletproof 5.3L V8 under the hood. Only 142,000 miles and it's been dealer-serviced its whole life. At $24,900, this thing won't last the weekend — I actually just had a guy call about it this morning. You looking for something for the family?

speaker: buyer

args.message: I appreciate the enthusiasm. Let me dig into the actual service history and condition. First: you mentioned dealer-serviced its whole life — I need specifics. Can you provide documented records showing every service visit, oil change intervals, and major work done? What was the service schedule like, and do you have receipts?

text: I appreciate the enthusiasm. Let me dig into the actual service history and condition. First: you mentioned dealer-serviced its whole life — I need specifics. Can you provide documented records showing every service visit, oil change intervals, and major work done? What was the service schedule like, and do you have receipts?

speaker: seller

args.message:
Absolutely, I love a thorough buyer — shows you know what you're doing. So here's what we've got: the previous owner was meticulous, brought it into the Chevy dealer every 5,000 miles like clockwork for oil changes and tire rotations. We have records showing the transmission fluid was serviced at 60,000, coolant flush at 80,000, and spark plugs done around 100,000 — all the major interval stuff you'd want to see on a well-maintained V8.

Now, I'll be straight with you — we don't have every single receipt going back to 2016 in a neat little folder, but we do have the CarFax showing consistent dealer service entries, and I can print that out for you right now. The important thing is there's no gaps, no sketchy independent shops, just dealer stamps. That's actually pretty rare on a vehicle this age. Want me to grab that CarFax printout while you take a closer look?

args.message: Good — yes, I want to see that CarFax. But before you grab it, a few more specifics: Has this vehicle ever been in any accidents, even minor ones? Any frame damage, flood damage, or title issues? And when you say "consistent dealer service entries" — are there any service gaps longer than, say, 6-7 months between visits? Also, what was the previous owner's name or ownership tenure — how long did they own it?

text: Good — yes, I want to see that CarFax. But before you grab it, a few more specifics: Has this vehicle ever been in any accidents, even minor ones? Any frame damage, flood damage, or title issues? And when you say "consistent dealer service entries" — are there any service gaps longer than, say, 6-7 months between visits? Also, what was the previous owner's name or ownership tenure — how long did they own it?

speaker: seller

args.message:
Great questions — you're doing your due diligence and I respect that. So let me knock these out for you:

Accidents: Clean. No accidents reported on the CarFax, no frame damage, no flood history. Title is clean as a whistle — no salvage, no rebuilt, nothing like that. This is a straight, honest truck.

Service gaps: Nothing concerning. The longest gap I recall seeing was maybe 8 months at one point, but that was during COVID when nobody was driving much — mileage barely moved during that window, so it tracks. Otherwise, pretty consistent every 5-6 months.

Previous ownership: This was a two-owner vehicle. First owner had it for about four years, second owner — a gentleman here locally — had it for the last four. He traded it in on a new Suburban, wanted the updated interior. Family guy, used it for road trips and hauling the kids around. Exactly the kind of ownership history you want on these Tahoes.

Let me go grab that CarFax and you can see it all laid out. Give me two minutes — feel free to pop the hood, check out the interior, whatever you'd like.

speaker: buyer

args.message: Thanks. Before you step away, one more thing: You mentioned the second owner traded it in on a new Suburban. When did that happen — how long has this Tahoe been on your lot? And what condition is the engine in right now — any leaks, corrosion, or unusual sounds when running? Also, has anything needed repair or replacement in the last 12 months, even if it was dealer-serviced?

text: Thanks. Before you step away, one more thing: You mentioned the second owner traded it in on a new Suburban. When did that happen — how long has this Tahoe been on your lot? And what condition is the engine in right now — any leaks, corrosion, or unusual sounds when running? Also, has anything needed repair or replacement in the last 12 months, even if it was dealer-serviced?

speaker: seller

args.message:
Good timing on this one, actually — we just got it about three weeks ago. Fresh trade-in, went through our inspection, detailed it up, and put it on the lot. Hasn't been sitting around collecting dust, that's for sure.

Engine condition: Solid. That 5.3L V8 is one of the most reliable engines GM has ever built — these things run forever with basic maintenance. No leaks that we found during inspection, no corrosion under the hood. Starts right up, runs smooth. You'll hear that V8 rumble but that's just the nature of the beast — it's a truck motor, you know? Nothing out of the ordinary.

As for recent repairs: the previous owner actually just had the brakes done about 10 months ago — new pads and rotors all around — and fresh tires maybe 15,000 miles back. So you're not walking into any immediate maintenance expenses, which is a nice bonus on a vehicle at this price point.

Tell you what, let me grab those keys too and you can fire it up yourself, hear how it runs. Nothing beats getting behind the wheel. Be right back.

And when you say the brakes were done 10 months ago — do you have documentation of that work, or is that just what the previous owner told you?

speaker: seller

args.message:
Good eye for detail — I like it. So the tires are Michelin Defender LTX, which is a solid all-season tire for these SUVs. Tread depth is looking good, I'd say 6/32 to 7/32 across the board — plenty of life left, easily another 20,000-25,000 miles before you're thinking about replacing them. No uneven wear, which tells you the alignment's been kept up.

On the brakes: that one came from the trade-in paperwork the previous owner provided. He had the receipt from the Chevy dealer — I believe it was actually in the glovebox with some other documents when we took it in. Let me check if it's still in there when I grab the keys, or it might be in our file. Either way, you can feel it when you drive it — brakes are tight and responsive, no pulsing, no noise. 

Alright, give me just a minute — I'll grab the CarFax, the keys, and see if I can dig up that brake receipt for you. Sit tight!

speaker: buyer

args.message: Appreciate it. While you're pulling those together, let me ask about the interior and any known issues: How are the leather seats holding up — any cracking, staining, or wear? What about the Bose audio system — does everything work? Any issues with the climate control, power windows, locks, or other electronics? And has anything in the cabin ever needed repair or replacement?

text: Appreciate it. While you're pulling those together, let me ask about the interior and any known issues: How are the leather seats holding up — any cracking, staining, or wear? What about the Bose audio system — does everything work? Any issues with the climate control, power windows, locks, or other electronics? And has anything in the cabin ever needed repair or replacement?

speaker: seller

args.message:
Interior is honestly one of the highlights on this one — you'll see when you get in there. The leather seats are in great shape, maybe some very minor creasing on the driver's bolster from entry and exit, but that's totally normal wear on any vehicle this age. No cracking, no tears, no stains. Second row and third row look barely used — probably because the kids were back there and not the adults, right?

Bose system sounds fantastic. All speakers working, the subwoofer in the back has that nice low-end punch, Bluetooth pairs up instantly. I actually played some music through it when we were detailing it — no complaints there.

Climate control is fully functional, blows cold up front and in the rear. Dual-zone works like it should. Power windows, locks, mirrors — all operating smooth, no slow windows or sticky locks. Liftgate goes up and down no problem.

Electronics-wise, the infotainment screen is responsive, backup camera is crystal clear, no warning lights on the dash. These Tahoes are pretty bulletproof on the electrical side once you're past the first couple model years — 2016 was when they really had everything dialed in.

As far as repairs inside, nothing that I'm aware of. This one was clearly well cared for. Alright, let me go grab those docs and keys — be right back!

speaker: buyer

args.message: Thanks. One final set before you head off: How many previous owners does the title show — you said two, but confirm that matches the title document. And are you the selling dealer, or are you a private party? Also, what's your warranty or return policy on this vehicle, if any?

text: Thanks. One final set before you head off: How many previous owners does the title show — you said two, but confirm that matches the title document. And are you the selling dealer, or are you a private party? Also, what's your warranty or return policy on this vehicle, if any?

speaker: seller

args.message:
You got it — yes, two owners on the title, that matches what CarFax shows and what the title document reflects. Clean chain of ownership, no weird flips or auction bouncing around.

We're a licensed dealer, so you've got the peace of mind that comes with that — we handle all the title transfer, registration paperwork, everything's done properly and above board. No DMV headaches for you.

As for warranty: the factory powertrain warranty is long gone at this mileage, but we do offer optional extended service contracts if you want that extra coverage. We partner with a reputable third-party warranty company — bumper to bumper or powertrain only, your choice, various term lengths. A lot of folks find it's worth it on a full-size SUV just for peace of mind.

Return policy: we do a 3-day/300-mile exchange policy. If something's not right for you, you can bring it back and swap into something else on the lot. We're not one of those places that disappears once you drive off — we want repeat customers, referrals, all that. We stand behind what we sell.

Alright, I'll be right back with everything. Take your time looking it over!

Alright, I'll be right back with everything. Take your time looking it over!

speaker: buyer

args.message: Got it. One more clarification on the warranty: when you say "optional extended service contracts" — what's the cost for that, and what are the actual coverage terms? Specifically, what's *excluded*? I want to know what I'm actually buying, not marketing language. And before I get behind the wheel, I want to see that CarFax printout and any service records you have in hand right now.

text: Got it. One more clarification on the warranty: when you say "optional extended service contracts" — what's the cost for that, and what are the actual coverage terms? Specifically, what's *excluded*? I want to know what I'm actually buying, not marketing language. And before I get behind the wheel, I want to see that CarFax printout and any service records you have in hand right now.

speaker: seller

args.message:
Fair enough — I'll give you the straight talk. The extended service contracts typically run anywhere from $1,500 to $3,000 depending on the coverage level and term length. For a vehicle like this, you're probably looking at a 2-year/24,000-mile or 3-year/36,000-mile plan.

What's covered on the better plans: engine internals, transmission, transfer case, drivetrain components, AC compressor, electrical modules, that kind of stuff. The powertrain-only plans are cheaper but obviously more limited.

What's excluded — and this is where they get you, so good question: wear-and-tear items like brakes, rotors, tires, wiper blades, bulbs. Also typically excludes pre-existing conditions, so anything that fails in the first 30 days can sometimes get scrutinized. Seals and gaskets are often only covered if they fail along with an internal component. Suspension bushings, shocks, struts — usually not covered. And there's always a deductible, typically $100 per visit.

Honestly? On a well-maintained Tahoe like this, a lot of people skip it and just pocket that money for a rainy day. But it's there if you want it.

Now — let me actually go grab that CarFax and whatever service docs we have on file. I've kept you waiting long enough. Two minutes, I promise. You want a water or coffee while I'm up?

speaker: buyer

args.message: I'm all set, thanks. Go ahead and grab those documents. While you're doing that, I want to be direct: given that this is a $24,900 vehicle with 142,000 miles and no remaining factory warranty, I'm going to request an independent inspection before I make any offer. It's just due diligence on my end — nothing personal. I'll be looking at the engine, transmission, and overall body condition. I'll handle that separately, but I wanted to give you a heads-up so there's no surprises.

text: I'm all set, thanks. Go ahead and grab those documents. While you're doing that, I want to be direct: given that this is a $24,900 vehicle with 142,000 miles and no remaining factory warranty, I'm going to request an independent inspection before I make any offer. It's just due diligence on my end — nothing personal. I'll be looking at the engine, transmission, and overall body condition. I'll handle that separately, but I wanted to give you a heads-up so there's no surprises.

speaker: seller

args.message:
Hey, absolutely — no offense taken at all. That's a smart move, and honestly it tells me you're a serious buyer, not a tire-kicker. I'd do the same thing on a purchase this size.

You're welcome to take it to whoever you trust — your own mechanic, a Chevy dealer, one of those independent inspection services, whatever works for you. We've got nothing to hide here. Like I said, this one went through our own inspection when it came in, but I totally understand wanting a second set of eyes. That's just good due diligence.

Only thing I'd ask is we schedule that sooner rather than later — like I mentioned, there's been some interest on this one and I'd hate for you to miss out while waiting on an appointment. If your guy can get you in within the next day or two, that'd be ideal. We can even leave a deposit to hold it if you want that peace of mind while you get the inspection done.

Let me go grab those docs now. When I come back, we can talk logistics — maybe you take it for a test drive first, get a feel for it, and then set up your inspection from there. Sound good?

speaker: buyer

args.message: Understood on the timeline — I'll move fast once I see the paperwork. But before you go: what's your deposit policy? How much, is it refundable if the inspection turns up issues, and what happens to it if I decide not to proceed after the inspection? I need to know the terms upfront.

text: Understood on the timeline — I'll move fast once I see the paperwork. But before you go: what's your deposit policy? How much, is it refundable if the inspection turns up issues, and what happens to it if I decide not to proceed after the inspection? I need to know the terms upfront.

speaker: seller

args.message:
Totally fair — let me lay it out clearly for you.

Standard deposit to hold a vehicle is $500. That takes it off the lot and off our website so nobody else can swoop in while you're doing your due diligence.

Here's how it works: If you move forward with the purchase, that $500 goes directly toward your purchase price — it's not an extra fee. If the inspection turns up something material that we didn't disclose or that changes the deal, you get your deposit back, no questions asked. We're not in the business of holding people hostage over a deposit when something legitimate comes up.

Now, if you just change your mind — like, inspection comes back clean but you decide you'd rather have a Suburban or you found something else — that's where it gets a little grayer. Typically we'd work with you, maybe apply it toward another vehicle on the lot, but a straight refund for cold feet isn't guaranteed. That's only fair since we've taken it off the market for you.

But honestly? I'm not worried. Once your mechanic gets under this thing and sees how solid it is, I think you're gonna feel good about it. 

Alright — documents. I'm going. Back in two minutes!

Alright — documents. I'm going. Back in two minutes!

speaker: buyer

args.message: Got it — I understand the deposit terms. One thing before you go though: I want to be clear on what "material" means. If the inspection finds deferred maintenance, rust, transmission issues, engine wear beyond normal, or any safety concerns — those would qualify as material, correct? I'm not trying to be difficult, I just want that definition locked down so there's no argument later.

text: Got it — I understand the deposit terms. One thing before you go though: I want to be clear on what "material" means. If the inspection finds deferred maintenance, rust, transmission issues, engine wear beyond normal, or any safety concerns — those would qualify as material, correct? I'm not trying to be difficult, I just want that definition locked down so there's no argument later.

speaker: seller

args.message:
No, that's a completely reasonable question and I'm glad you're pinning it down. Let me be clear:

Yes — if your inspection finds any of the following, that would absolutely qualify as material and your deposit would be fully refundable:

- Engine issues beyond normal wear: internal problems, significant oil consumption, misfires, anything that would require major repair
- Transmission problems: slipping, harsh shifts, shudder, anything indicating internal failure
- Rust or corrosion: anything structural or safety-related, frame damage, crossmember issues
- Safety concerns: suspension problems, brake system issues beyond normal wear, steering components
- Deferred maintenance: anything major that should have been done and wasn't, that would cost you significant money to address

Basically, if your mechanic comes back and says "this vehicle has a problem that wasn't disclosed and it's going to cost real money to fix," you're covered. I'm not going to sit here and argue semantics with you over a $500 deposit — that's not how we do business.

What wouldn't count: your mechanic saying "well, it's an 8-year-old truck with miles on it, things are worn." Normal wear and tear on a used vehicle isn't a gotcha moment, you know? But actual mechanical issues, rust problems, safety concerns — those are legitimate, and you'd get your money back.

We good on that? Alright — documents, keys, CarFax. Going now!
